title: Why joy feels suspicious after prolonged suffering
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The knot in your chest when something good happens 

 You get a compliment at work. Something genuine. Someone notices your effort. And instead of warmth...
htmlContent:
The knot in your chest when something good happens

You get a compliment at work. Something genuine. Someone notices your effort. And instead of warmth spreading through your chest, you feel a knot tighten. Your shoulders go up. Your breath gets shallow. You deflect. You minimize. You change the subject.

I know this knot. I've lived in it for years.

Here's the thing. When you've spent a long time in survival mode, your nervous system learns one language fluently: the language of threat. It becomes a specialist in scanning for danger. It knows how to brace for impact. It can predict betrayal from three rooms away. But joy? Joy is a foreign dialect. Your system doesn't trust it. It sounds like a trap.

Think about it. If you grew up in an environment where good moments were followed by something terrible, your brain made a logical calculation. Happiness is a setup. Relief is a trick. That moment of peace? It's just the quiet before the next shoe drops. Your nervous system isn't stupid. It's trying to protect you. It's just working with old data.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching how animals in the wild shake off trauma and return to normal functioning. But humans? We get stuck. The energy of the threat gets trapped in our bodies. And here's what's wild. The same mechanism that keeps us frozen in fear also keeps us frozen against joy. Your system can't tell the difference between a real lion and a memory of one. And it can't tell the difference between a genuine moment of happiness and a trap baited with warmth.

So you sit there, receiving a compliment, and your body screams: Run. This is dangerous. Do not let your guard down.

That's not a character flaw. That's a survival strategy that outlived its usefulness.

The brain's accounting error with happiness

Dan Siegel, the clinical psychiatrist who coined the term interpersonal neurobiology, talks about how the brain integrates experience. When things go well, the brain should be able to file that under "safe" and "good." But after prolonged suffering, the filing system breaks.

Your brain starts keeping a ledger. And it's heavily weighted toward loss. You remember every betrayal. Every disappointment. Every time you let yourself hope and got crushed. Your brain is a meticulous accountant of pain. But it barely registers the good moments. They slip through like water through a sieve.

This isn't your fault. It's how the brain evolved. Negative experiences demand more processing. They require storage for future avoidance. Positive experiences? They're nice, but they don't threaten survival. So the brain files them away carelessly. It's an evolutionary bias that kept our ancestors alive. But it makes modern recovery a slog.

I remember the first time someone told me I deserved good things. I almost laughed in their face. Not because I was being difficult. Because the idea was so foreign to my internal experience that it felt like they were speaking a different language. My body didn't have a reference point for "deserving good things." It had reference points for "surviving bad things." That was it. That was the whole library.

When joy feels suspicious, what's really happening is a mismatch. Your present moment reality says something positive is occurring. But your nervous system's memory banks say something positive is a precursor to something negative. The system doesn't trust the data. It defaults to caution. It defaults to withdrawal.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work on this is quietly . She doesn't ask you to force yourself to feel joy. She asks you to notice the resistance. To name it. To sit with the part of you that says you don't deserve this. That's where the real work starts.

Stay with me here. The goal isn't to bulldoze through your suspicion. The goal is to make friends with it. To understand that your suspicion is a protector. It's the part of you that kept you alive through terrible things. It deserves gratitude, not shame.

The body's refusal to land in safety

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, gave us a framework that changed how I understand this. He talks about the ventral vagal state. That's the state of safety, connection, and social engagement. It's the state where joy is possible. Where laughter comes easily. Where you can rest.

But here's the problem. If your nervous system has been stuck in sympathetic activation (fight or flight) or dorsal vagal shutdown (freeze or collapse) for years, the ventral vagal state doesn't feel safe. It feels wrong. It feels exposed. Your body has forgotten what safety feels like. Or maybe it never learned in the first place.

I see this in my own body constantly. I'll have a genuinely good day. Things go well. I feel a flicker of something that might be contentment. And then, without any conscious thought, my body finds a problem. It picks a fight. It worries about something that hasn't happened. It finds a reason to be anxious. Because anxiety is familiar. Anxiety is a worn path in the brain. Joy is an overgrown trail that I haven't walked in years.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," showed us that trauma is not just a story in your head. It's a physiological reality. Your body holds the memory. Your muscles remember the tension. Your gut remembers the dread. Your skin remembers the flinch. When something good happens, your body doesn't automatically switch into a new gear. It stays in the gear it knows. The survival gear.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model gives you a way to talk to the parts of you that reject joy. Not to silence them. To understand them. To let them know you see their effort.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something soften? Did something tighten? That's the conversation you need to have. Not the intellectual conversation. The bodily one.

Recovery from prolonged suffering isn't about thinking your way into joy. It's about teaching your body, millimeter by millimeter, that safety is possible. That joy is not a trap. That you can land in a good moment and not be destroyed.

This takes time. It takes repetition. It takes patience with the parts of you that still don't believe it.

The slow work of letting joy in

I'm not going to tell you to just "choose joy." That's toxic positivity dressed up in a party hat. If you could choose joy, you would have done it already. The problem isn't that you're not trying hard enough. The problem is that your nervous system is running a different operating system.

What works is slower than that. Much slower.

What works is noticing the flicker. That moment when something almost feels good. And instead of running from it, you just stay. You don't grab at it. You don't try to make it bigger. You just let it exist for a second. And if it goes away, that's fine. You try again tomorrow.

What works is telling the suspicious part of you: I hear you. I know you're scared. We don't have to trust this completely. We can just let it sit here for a moment and see what happens.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Nedra Glover Tawwab's work is important here because boundaries aren't just about keeping bad things out. They're about letting good things in. If you can't say no to what drains you, you can't fully say yes to what fills you. The two are connected.

I've been practicing this for years now. And I still have days where joy feels like a stranger at the door. But the difference is, I don't slam the door anymore. I crack it open. I let the stranger stand there for a minute. I don't invite them in for dinner. But I don't chase them away either.

That's progress. That's the slow work.

Your suspicion of joy is not a failure. It's a scar. And scars are not weaknesses. They're evidence of healing that already happened. They're tissue that grew back differently because it had to.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey's conversation shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." And that shift is everything. It takes the shame out of the equation. It makes your suspicion of joy make sense.

You're not broken. You're adapted. And what adapted to survive can learn to adapt to thrive. It just takes time. And gentleness. And a willingness to let joy be small at first.

Let it be small. Let it be a single note instead of a symphony. Let it be a moment of peace instead of a lifetime of happiness. Let it be enough that you didn't run today. That you stayed present for three seconds of something that felt okay.

That's not nothing. That's everything.

Your nervous system will learn. It will slowly, reluctantly, begin to trust that good things can stay. That not every moment of peace is a setup. That joy can coexist with memory. That you can hold both the pain of the past and the possibility of the present in the same body.

It's not a straight line. It's not a clean arc. It's a messy, recursive, frustrating, beautiful process. And you're already in it. You're reading this. You're thinking about it. You're noticing the knot in your chest when something good happens. That's the beginning.

Stay with it. Stay with yourself. The joy will come. Not all at once. Not perfectly. But it will come. And when it does, you'll recognize it. Not because it feels familiar. But because it feels like a door you chose to open instead of a door you were pushed through.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
